blocks.text: Systems Biology: In-Depth Review

lines.text: Systems Biology: In-Depth
Review

blocks.text: Project 2359 Stress Test Deck

lines.text: Project 2359 Stress Test Deck

blocks.text: 1. Neurotransmission Mechanisms

lines.text: 1. Neurotransmission Mechanisms

blocks.text: • Primary Mechanism: The integration of neurotransmission mechanisms relies heavily on protein-protein interactions, ATP-dependent phosphorylation, and concentration gradients across the semi-permeable lipid bilayer. These processes are extremely sensitive to temperature and pH variations.

lines.text: • Primary Mechanism: The integration of neurotransmission mechanisms relies heavily on
protein-protein interactions, ATP-dependent phosphorylation, and concentration gradients across
the semi-permeable lipid bilayer. These processes are extremely sensitive to temperature and
pH variations.

blocks.text: • Enzymatic Regulation: Kinases and phosphatases dynamically alter the structural conformation of key channel proteins. For instance, the binding of cyclic AMP (cAMP) to the regulatory subunits of Protein Kinase A (PKA) releases the catalytic subunits.

lines.text: • Enzymatic Regulation: Kinases and phosphatases dynamically alter the structural
conformation of key channel proteins. For instance, the binding of cyclic AMP (cAMP) to the
regulatory subunits of Protein Kinase A (PKA) releases the catalytic subunits.

blocks.text: • Pathological Correlates: Dysregulation in this specific pathway often presents clinically as a spectrum of metabolic or neurological disorders. Genetic mutations affecting the transcription factors responsible for expressing these proteins result in truncated or misfolded polypeptides.

lines.text: • Pathological Correlates: Dysregulation in this specific pathway often presents clinically as a
spectrum of metabolic or neurological disorders. Genetic mutations affecting the transcription
factors responsible for expressing these proteins result in truncated or misfolded polypeptides.

blocks.text: • Pharmacological Intervention: Competitive inhibitors can bind to the active site, effectively halting the conversion of substrate to product. The Michaelis-Menten kinetics (Km and Vmax) are altered depending on whether the inhibition is competitive, non-competitive, or uncompetitive.

lines.text: • Pharmacological Intervention: Competitive inhibitors can bind to the active site, effectively
halting the conversion of substrate to product. The Michaelis-Menten kinetics (Km and Vmax)
are altered depending on whether the inhibition is competitive, non-competitive, or
uncompetitive.

blocks.text: • Energy Requirements: This is a highly endergonic process. The hydrolysis of ATP to ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi) yields approximately -7.3 kcal/mol of free energy, which is coupled to this reaction to drive it forward against thermodynamic equilibrium.

lines.text: • Energy Requirements: This is a highly endergonic process. The hydrolysis of ATP to ADP and
inorganic phosphate (Pi) yields approximately -7.3 kcal/mol of free energy, which is coupled to
this reaction to drive it forward against thermodynamic equilibrium.

blocks.text: • Cellular Localization: While the initial stages occur in the cytosol, the terminal enzymatic steps are tethered to the inner mitochondrial membrane, increasing the local concentration of intermediates and preventing their diffusion into off-target pathways. [Cite: "Advanced Physiology, Chapter 10"]

lines.text: • Cellular Localization: While the initial stages occur in the cytosol, the terminal enzymatic steps
are tethered to the inner mitochondrial membrane, increasing the local concentration of
intermediates and preventing their diffusion into off-target
pathways.
[Cite:
"Advanced Physiology, Chapter 10"]
